text: The surgeon performs a 4.5 cm incision located 2 cm to the right of the umbilicus, preparing the site for robotic-assisted laparoscopic access.

The surgeon places the applied medical gel port with a wound protector into the incision on the right side of the umbilicus, and docks the da Vinci SI robot onto the patient for the robotic-assisted laparoscopic procedure.
Both robotic arms are inserted into the peritoneal cavity through the single incision, crossing each other as the surgeon prepares to reassign arm control for the procedure.

The DaVinci monitor displays flipped indicators for robotic arms one and two, showing that the surgeon's right hand controls the robotic arm on the left side, while the left hand controls the arm on the right side.

The surgeon begins the operation by exposing and visualizing the vascular supply to the sigmoid colon. Using monopolar endoshears, the sigmoidal branches are carefully dissected free from the surrounding tissue in a medial to lateral approach.
The surgeon exposes and carefully skeletonizes the vessel, removing surrounding tissue to clearly delineate its structure.
The surgeon uses a five millimeter ligature device to seal and divide the largest vessels encountered during the dissection of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection of the mesosigmoid, creating an opening through the tissue to expose the opposite side.
The surgeon utilizes the window created in the mesentery as a point of retraction, enhancing exposure and facilitating traction and counter-traction during the dissection of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon makes a longitudinal incision along the craniocaudal axis of the operating field to enhance maneuverability and accommodate the robotic arms during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection around the rectosigmoid junction, following the previously established technique, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a five millimeter ligature device to seal and divide larger vessels encountered during the dissection, which cannot be safely coagulated with monopolar energy.

The surgeon performs dissection around the rectosigmoid junction, enhancing serosal contraposition between the distal descending colon and the rectal stump in preparation for an end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a 60mm roticulating automatic endostapler to divide the colon at the prepared retosigmoid junction, ensuring precise separation of the tissue.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the most distal point of the colon where the blood supply remains intact, ensuring a healthy and well-vascularized site for the upcoming anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares to fire a second reload of the stapler to complete the resection at the rectosigmoid junction, ensuring the colon is adequately divided.
The surgeon uses under shears to complete the resection of the colon, ensuring precise cutting and separation of the remaining tissue.
The surgeon uses a 5mm video scope with 30-degree lenses, positioned facing upward to optimize the visual field and accommodate robotic arm movements during the procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the anatomy to localize the ureter, a step common in both multiple port laparoscopic and open colectomy procedures.
The surgeon checks the mobilization of the descending colon by ensuring it reaches into the pelvis without tension, indicating sufficient length for the procedure.
The surgeon exteriorizes the colon through the single incision wound protector, transects the specimen, and places the anvil in the distal descending colon extracorporeally.

The surgeon reapplies the gel port on the wound protector and completes the EEA stapled anastomosis using regular laparoscopic instruments through the single port, without re-docking the robotic system.
The surgeon inspects the anastomotic donuts on the back table and performs an underwater leak test on the stapled line to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis.
